items.title: Why pituitary adenomas classically cause bitemporal hemianopia
items.content:
The optic chiasm sits directly above the pituitary gland. At the chiasm, fibers from the NASAL halves of each retina cross to the opposite side; these crossing fibers carry information from the TEMPORAL visual fields and travel through the ventral, most inferior part of the chiasm — exactly where an expanding sellar mass first presses.
• Crossing nasal fibers (temporal field information) are compressed first → bitemporal field loss.
• Because the inferior chiasm is affected first, the SUPERIOR temporal quadrants are lost before the inferior ones.
• If the compression is instead behind the chiasm (postchiasmal), the defect becomes homonymous rather than bitemporal.
keyPoints: Crossing nasal retinal fibers carry temporal visual field information and occupy the ventral chiasm.
Bitemporal hemianopia, superior quadrants first, is the classic finding of chiasmal compression from below.
Homonymous hemianopia instead suggests postchiasmal compression.
items.reference: Ophthalmologic Evaluation: "Bitemporal hemianopia, often more pronounced superiorly, is observed classically. It occurs because nasal ganglion cell fibers, which cross in the optic chiasm, are especially vulnerable to compression of the ventral optic chiasm."

items.title: How pituitary stalk compression produces the "stalk effect": early hyperprolactinemia then panhypopituitarism
items.content:
Dopamine, delivered from the hypothalamus via the portal vessels in the pituitary stalk, is a TONIC INHIBITOR of lactotrope prolactin secretion (unlike other anterior pituitary hormones, which depend on stimulatory releasing hormones).
• An expanding sellar mass first compresses the portal vessels in the stalk, cutting off dopamine delivery.
• Loss of dopamine inhibition → disinhibited PRL secretion → mild-to-moderate hyperprolactinemia (typically 30-100 µg/L), even though the mass itself may be hormonally "nonfunctioning."
• As compression worsens, delivery of the STIMULATORY releasing hormones (GnRH, GHRH, TRH, CRH) is also lost → progressive loss of gonadotropin, GH, TSH, and ACTH secretion.
• The same stalk-disruption mechanism (trauma, whiplash, skull-base fracture, suprasellar surgery) explains AVP deficiency from posterior pituitary/stalk involvement.
keyPoints: Dopamine is inhibitory for PRL — its loss RAISES prolactin (unlike the loss of other releasing hormones, which lowers their target hormones).
Mild/moderate PRL elevation (30-100 µg/L) from stalk compression should not be mistaken for a prolactinoma.
Progressive stalk damage subsequently causes loss of the other anterior pituitary axes.
items.reference: Local Mass Effects: "Pituitary stalk compression by a hormonally active or inactive intrasellar mass may compress the portal vessels, disrupting pituitary access to hypothalamic hormones and dopamine; this results in early hyperprolactinemia and later concurrent loss of other pituitary hormones."

items.title: Management of incidentally discovered pituitary masses
items.content:
Pituitary "incidentalomas" are found commonly on MRI obtained for unrelated reasons; most are adenomas.
• Small lesions with NO hormone hypersecretion: safe to monitor with serial MRI, performed annually and then less often if stable.
• Larger incidental MACROADENOMAS: consider resection, because roughly one-third become invasive or cause local pressure effects over time.
• If hormone hypersecretion IS identified (on the routine screening panel), specific medical or surgical therapy is indicated regardless of size.
• Masses >1 cm should also be evaluated for nonadenomatous etiologies (meningioma, craniopharyngioma, glioma) based on characteristic MRI features.
keyPoints: Small, non-hypersecreting incidentalomas: serial MRI surveillance (annual, then less frequent if stable).
Larger incidental macroadenomas: consider resection (~1/3 become invasive or symptomatic).
Any hormone hypersecretion mandates specific therapy regardless of lesion size.
items.reference: Magnetic Resonance Imaging: "In the absence of hormone hypersecretion, these small intrasellar lesions can be monitored safely with MRI, which is performed annually and then less often if there is no evidence of further growth. Resection should be considered for incidentally discovered larger macroadenomas, because about one-third become invasive or cause local pressure effects."

items.title: Why MRI is the imaging modality of choice for sellar lesions, with CT reserved for bone
items.content:
Gadolinium-enhanced T1-weighted MRI (sagittal and coronal) gives superior SOFT-TISSUE contrast, allowing clear delineation of the hypothalamus, stalk, pituitary tissue, suprasellar cisterns, cavernous sinuses, sphenoid sinus, and optic chiasm. Adenomas typically show LOWER signal than normal pituitary on T1 and HIGHER signal on T2, making them distinguishable from surrounding gland.
CT's relative strength is BONE and CALCIUM: it is reserved for defining the extent of bony erosion (e.g., through the sellar floor into the sphenoid sinus) or detecting calcification (e.g., in craniopharyngioma or meningioma) — information MRI does not provide as well.
keyPoints: MRI with gadolinium is the primary modality: best soft-tissue contrast for gland, stalk, chiasm, and cavernous sinus.
Adenomas: lower T1 signal, higher T2 signal than normal pituitary tissue.
CT is reserved for assessing bony erosion or calcification, not for primary characterization.
items.reference: Magnetic Resonance Imaging: "Computed tomography (CT) scan is reserved to define the extent of bony erosion or the presence of calcification."

items.title: How BRAF V600E-targeted therapy is changing management of papillary craniopharyngioma
items.content:
Craniopharyngiomas are benign but locally aggressive suprasellar masses derived from Rathke's pouch remnants. Surgery alone cures fewer than half of patients (tumor often adheres to vital structures), and without radiotherapy ~75% recur with 10-year survival <50%.
A subset, particularly the PAPILLARY histologic variant, harbors activating BRAF V600E mutations — the same driver mutation targeted in melanoma. This has opened a medical avenue:
• BRAF inhibitors (dabrafenib or vemurafenib), alone or combined with MEK inhibitors (trametinib or cobimetinib), have produced long-term growth responses in some patients.
• This offers an option for tumors not amenable to safe complete resection, complementing surgery and radiotherapy.
keyPoints: Papillary craniopharyngiomas often carry activating BRAF V600E mutations.
BRAF inhibitors ± MEK inhibitors can produce durable tumor responses.
Relevant because surgery alone cures <50% and recurrence without radiotherapy is ~75%.
items.reference: Sellar Masses: "As some craniopharyngiomas (particularly papillary) are associated with activated BRAF V600E mutations, use of BRAF inhibitors (dabrafenib or vemurafenib) either alone or in combination with MEK inhibitors (trametinib or cobimetinib) has resulted in long-term growth responses in some patients."

items.title: Why arginine vasopressin deficiency (diabetes insipidus) is the classic presentation of pituitary metastases
items.content:
The posterior pituitary receives blood directly from the hypophyseal arteries, whereas the anterior pituitary depends mainly on the portal venous system draining the hypothalamus. Bloodborne (hematogenous) metastatic deposits therefore seed almost exclusively the POSTERIOR pituitary.
• Because AVP is made in the hypothalamus and stored/released from the posterior pituitary, a posterior pituitary/stalk metastasis disrupts AVP release → AVP deficiency (central diabetes insipidus) is often the PRESENTING feature of lung, gastrointestinal, breast, and other cancers metastatic to the pituitary.
• Breast cancer accounts for about half of pituitary metastases, and roughly a quarter of patients with metastatic breast cancer harbor such deposits.
• Anterior pituitary insufficiency is rare and only occurs if stalk involvement is extensive.
keyPoints: The posterior pituitary has a direct arterial supply, making it the preferential site for hematogenous metastases.
AVP deficiency (diabetes insipidus) is often the presenting feature of pituitary metastasis.
Breast cancer is the source of about half of pituitary metastases.
items.reference: Sellar Masses: "Bloodborne metastatic deposits are found almost exclusively in the posterior pituitary. Accordingly, AVP-D can be a presenting feature of lung, gastrointestinal, breast, and other pituitary metastases."

items.title: Why GnRH-secreting hypothalamic hamartomas cause central precocious puberty with gelastic seizures
items.content:
Hypothalamic hamartomas are non-neoplastic masses of differentiated neurons that can ECTOPICALLY overexpress hypothalamic neuropeptides, including GnRH, GHRH, and CRH.
• A GnRH-producing hamartoma provides a continuous, ectopic source of GnRH drive to the pituitary gonadotropes, prematurely activating the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis → central precocious puberty.
• Because the hamartoma is composed of aberrant neuronal tissue, it can also generate abnormal electrical discharges → laughing-associated (gelastic) seizures and psychomotor delay.
• Paradoxically, TREATMENT uses long-acting GnRH ANALOGUES — continuous (rather than pulsatile) receptor stimulation desensitizes pituitary gonadotropes, suppressing gonadotropin secretion and halting pubertal progression.
• When combined with craniofacial anomalies, imperforate anus, and cardiac/renal/lung defects, this constellation is Pallister-Hall syndrome (GLI3 mutation).
keyPoints: Hamartomas may ectopically secrete GnRH, GHRH, or CRH.
GnRH-producing hamartomas cause precocious puberty, psychomotor delay, and gelastic seizures.
Long-acting GnRH analogues paradoxically SUPPRESS gonadotropins via receptor desensitization.
Pallister-Hall syndrome (GLI3 mutation) links hamartomas to craniofacial/anorectal/cardiac/renal anomalies.
items.reference: Sellar Masses: "With GnRH-producing tumors, children present with precocious puberty, psychomotor delay, and laughing-associated seizures. Medical treatment of GnRH-producing hamartomas with long-acting GnRH analogues effectively suppresses gonadotropin secretion and controls premature pubertal development."

items.title: How activating Gα mutations drive somatotrope adenoma formation in acromegaly
items.content:
Normal physiology: GHRH binds its Gs-protein-coupled receptor on somatotropes → activates adenylyl cyclase → raises cyclic AMP → stimulates both GH secretion and somatotrope cell proliferation.
In ~35% of GH-secreting adenomas, somatic ("gsp") mutations in Gα attenuate its INTRINSIC GTPase activity — the molecular "off switch" that normally terminates the signal.
• The G protein remains locked in its active state → cyclic AMP stays constitutively elevated.
• Sustained cAMP induces the transcription factor Pit-1 and activates CREB.
• The result is unchecked somatotrope proliferation and GH hypersecretion — autonomous and independent of circulating GHRH levels.
keyPoints: GHRH receptor signals via Gαs → cAMP → CREB to drive GH secretion AND somatotrope proliferation.
~35% of GH-secreting adenomas have Gα mutations that impair the GTPase 'off switch'.
This produces constitutive, GHRH-independent cAMP signaling, explaining autonomous tumor growth and hormone excess.
items.reference: Pathogenesis: "A subset (~35%) of GH-secreting pituitary tumors contains sporadic mutations in Gα. These mutations attenuate intrinsic GTPase activity, resulting in constitutive elevation of cyclic AMP, Pit-1 induction, and activation of cyclic AMP response element binding protein (CREB), thereby promoting somatotrope cell proliferation and GH secretion."

items.title: Familial syndromes associated with pituitary tumors
items.content:
Several inherited syndromes predispose to pituitary adenomas, each with a distinct molecular basis:
• MEN1 (autosomal dominant; inactivating germline mutation in MENIN, a tumor-suppressor on chromosome 11q13) → predisposes to parathyroid, pancreatic islet, AND pituitary adenomas. About half of MEN1 patients develop prolactinomas; acromegaly and Cushing's disease are less common.
• Carney complex (subset with PRKAR1A mutations, the R1α regulatory subunit of protein kinase A) → spotty skin pigmentation, myxomas, and endocrine tumors (testicular, adrenal, pituitary); acromegaly occurs in ~20%.
• McCune-Albright syndrome (postzygotic mosaic Gα mutations) → polyostotic fibrous dysplasia, pigmented skin patches, and endocrine hyperfunction including acromegaly, adrenal adenomas, and autonomous ovarian function.
• Familial acromegaly/gigantism: a subset of families carry germline mutations in AIP (aryl hydrocarbon receptor interacting protein).
keyPoints: MEN1 → MENIN mutation; prolactinoma is the most common pituitary tumor (~50% of patients).
Carney complex → PRKAR1A mutation; acromegaly in ~20%.
McCune-Albright → mosaic postzygotic Gα activation; fibrous dysplasia + endocrinopathies.
Familial acromegaly/gigantism → germline AIP mutations.
items.reference: Genetic Syndromes Associated with Pituitary Tumors: "About half of affected patients develop prolactinomas; acromegaly and Cushing's disease are less commonly encountered."

items.title: Why McCune-Albright syndrome produces a patchy, mosaic pattern of endocrine hyperfunction
items.content:
The activating Gα mutation responsible for McCune-Albright syndrome arises POSTZYGOTICALLY — i.e., after fertilization, during early embryonic development — rather than being present in the germline.
• Only the descendants of the cell in which the mutation first occurred carry it, producing a MOSAIC distribution of mutant versus normal cells across tissues.
• Mutant-lineage cells show constitutive cAMP signaling → autonomous hormone secretion (e.g., GH, cortisol, estrogen) and fibrous dysplasia where they reside in bone.
• Unaffected lineages function normally.
• This explains the characteristically PATCHY ("polyostotic"), ASYMMETRIC distribution of skin pigmentation, bone lesions, and endocrine gland involvement — a single germline mutation would instead produce a generalized/symmetric phenotype.
keyPoints: Postzygotic (somatic, not germline) Gα mutation → mosaicism.
Affected cell lineages show constitutive cAMP signaling → autonomous hormone production / fibrous dysplasia.
Mosaicism explains the asymmetric, patchy distribution of skin and bone findings, unlike a germline mutation.
items.reference: Genetic Syndromes Associated with Pituitary Tumors: "Hormonal hypersecretion results from constitutive cyclic AMP production caused by inactivation of the GTPase activity of Gα. The Gα mutations occur postzygotically, leading to a mosaic pattern of mutant expression."

items.title: Why a PRL level >200 µg/L is essentially diagnostic of a prolactinoma
items.content:
Almost every non-tumor cause of hyperprolactinemia — physiologic (pregnancy, lactation, stress), drug-induced (antipsychotics, metoclopramide, verapamil), stalk compression by a nonfunctioning mass, hypothyroidism, or renal failure — produces only MODEST elevations, typically <100 µg/L (often in the 30-100 range).
In contrast, a lactotrope adenoma secretes PRL autonomously and roughly in PROPORTION to its cell mass/tumor size. Only a true prolactinoma generates enough excess lactotrope tissue to push PRL above 200 µg/L (and values >250 usually indicate a macroadenoma).
Therefore, once physiologic, drug-related, and hypothyroid causes are excluded, a PRL >200 µg/L points strongly to a prolactinoma — and roughly tracks with how large it is.
keyPoints: Non-tumor causes of hyperprolactinemia rarely exceed 100 µg/L.
PRL level roughly correlates with prolactinoma size; >250 µg/L usually implies a macroadenoma.
Always exclude physiologic, drug-induced, and hypothyroid causes before attributing hyperprolactinemia to a tumor.
items.reference: Hyperprolactinemia: "Hyperprolactinemia >200 µg/L almost invariably is indicative of a prolactin-secreting pituitary adenoma. Physiologic causes, hypothyroidism, and drug-induced hyperprolactinemia should be excluded before extensive evaluation."

items.title: How assay artifacts can cause both falsely HIGH and falsely LOW PRL results
items.content:
Two opposite pitfalls can mislead the diagnosis and management of hyperprolactinemia:
• MACROPROLACTINEMIA: PRL can circulate as large aggregates (often PRL bound to IgG) that are biologically INACTIVE but are still recognized and measured by the immunoassay. This produces a falsely HIGH measured PRL in a patient with no symptoms and no tumor.
• "HOOK EFFECT": at extremely high TRUE PRL concentrations (>1000 µg/L, as seen with large macroprolactinomas), the two-site (sandwich) immunoassay becomes SATURATED and paradoxically reports a falsely LOW value. Serial sample dilution is required to reveal the true, very high level.
Clinically, suspect a hook effect when a large pituitary mass is associated with only a modestly elevated PRL — the apparent mismatch is the clue.
keyPoints: Macroprolactinemia → aggregated, biologically inactive PRL → falsely HIGH measured PRL.
Hook effect → extremely elevated true PRL saturates the assay → falsely LOW result; dilute the sample to correct.
Suspect the hook effect when a large pituitary mass shows only modest PRL elevation.
items.reference: Laboratory Investigation: "In patients with markedly elevated PRL levels (>1000 µg/L), reported results may be falsely lowered because of assay artifacts; sample dilution is required to measure these high values accurately. Falsely elevated values may be caused by aggregated forms of circulating PRL, which are usually biologically inactive (macroprolactinemia)."

items.title: Why men with prolactinomas tend to present with larger tumors than women
items.content:
In women, hyperprolactinemia produces EARLY, conspicuous symptoms — oligomenorrhea/amenorrhea, infertility, and galactorrhea — that prompt evaluation while the tumor is still a microadenoma (female:male ratio for microprolactinomas is ~20:1).
In men, the corresponding effects of hyperprolactinemia-induced hypogonadism (decreased libido, impotence, infertility) are LESS conspicuous, less often reported promptly, or attributed to other causes. Diagnosis is therefore often delayed until MASS-EFFECT symptoms (headache, visual field defects) develop — by which point the tumor has grown into a macroadenoma (female:male ratio for macroadenomas is near 1:1).
keyPoints: Female hypogonadal symptoms (amenorrhea, galactorrhea, infertility) prompt earlier diagnosis → microadenomas predominate in women.
Male hypogonadal symptoms are less conspicuous → diagnosis is often delayed until macroadenoma mass effects occur.
F:M ratio is ~20:1 for microprolactinomas but near 1:1 for macroadenomas.
items.reference: Prolactinoma — Etiology and Prevalence: "Men tend to present with larger tumors than women, possibly because the features of male hypogonadism are less readily evident."

items.title: Dopamine agonists are first-line therapy for prolactinoma
items.content:
Unlike most other pituitary adenomas (where surgery is first-line), DOPAMINE AGONISTS are the mainstay of treatment for prolactinomas of any size — they suppress PRL secretion AND synthesis AND lactotrope cell proliferation, so the tumor itself shrinks.
• Cabergoline: long-acting, high D2-receptor affinity, dosed 0.5-1.0 mg twice weekly. Achieves normoprolactinemia and restores gonadal function in ~80% of microadenomas; galactorrhea improves/resolves in 90%. Shrinks ~70% of macroprolactinomas. Generally better tolerated than bromocriptine.
• Bromocriptine: short-acting, started low (0.625-1.25 mg at bedtime with food) and titrated; most patients controlled on <7.5 mg/day.
• ~20% of patients (more often men) are dopamine-RESISTANT, often due to decreased D2 receptor numbers or a postreceptor defect.
Surgery is reserved for dopamine resistance/intolerance or a vision-threatening macroadenoma that fails to improve on drug therapy.
keyPoints: Dopamine agonists suppress PRL secretion AND shrink the tumor — a unique 'medical-first' approach among pituitary adenomas.
Cabergoline: long-acting, better tolerated, generally preferred for chronic management.
Bromocriptine: short-acting, preferred when conception is planned.
~20% of patients (especially men) are dopamine-resistant.
items.reference: Prolactinoma — Treatment: "Oral dopamine agonists (cabergoline and bromocriptine) are the mainstay of therapy for patients with micro- or macroprolactinomas. Dopamine agonists suppress PRL secretion and synthesis as well as lactotrope proliferation."

items.title: Why bromocriptine, not cabergoline, is preferred when pregnancy is desired in prolactinoma
items.content:
Bromocriptine is SHORT-ACTING and has >30 years of accumulated safety data with no evidence of teratogenicity. The usual strategy is: use mechanical contraception through three regular cycles to time conception, discontinue bromocriptine once pregnancy is confirmed, and follow PRL/symptoms clinically thereafter (microadenomas rarely enlarge during pregnancy — only ~5% do, versus 15-30% of macroadenomas).
Cabergoline's LONG half-life and HIGH D2-receptor affinity mean it lingers in the body (and crosses to the fetus) much longer after the last dose, and far less reproductive-safety data exist for it. For this reason, despite being generally preferred for long-term management because of fewer side effects, cabergoline is NOT recommended when fertility is the immediate goal.
keyPoints: Bromocriptine: short-acting, extensive pregnancy safety data, easy to stop once pregnancy is confirmed.
Cabergoline: long-acting/high-affinity → prolonged fetal exposure and less safety data → avoided when fertility is desired.
Microadenomas rarely grow during pregnancy (~5%) versus macroadenomas (15-30%), guiding monitoring intensity.
items.reference: Pregnancy: "Because cabergoline is long-acting with a high D2-receptor affinity, it is not recommended for use in women when fertility is desired."

items.title: Side effects of dopamine agonists and cardiac valve monitoring with cabergoline
items.content:
Common, dose-related side effects (improve with dose reduction): constipation, nasal stuffiness, dry mouth, nightmares, insomnia, vertigo. A "first-dose effect" of nausea, vomiting, and postural hypotension with faintness occurs in ~25% of patients. Rare effects include psychiatric symptoms (hallucinations, impulse control disorders, mood swings) in up to 5%, and rarely leukopenia, thrombocytopenia, pleural fibrosis, arrhythmias, and hepatitis.
CARDIAC VALVE REGURGITATION has been reported with cabergoline ≥3 mg/day — the dose range used for PARKINSON'S disease. However, large studies (>500 patients) at standard PROLACTINOMA doses (up to 2 mg/week) show NO increased valvulopathy. Nonetheless, because controlled prospective pituitary-population data are lacking, a baseline echocardiogram before starting standard-dose cabergoline is considered prudent.
keyPoints: Most side effects are dose-related and improve with slow titration or dose reduction.
Valvulopathy risk is established at high Parkinson's-range cabergoline doses (≥3 mg/d), NOT at standard prolactinoma doses (≤2 mg/week).
A baseline echocardiogram is still considered prudent before standard-dose cabergoline.
items.reference: Side Effects: "Patients with Parkinson disease who receive at least 3 mg of cabergoline daily have been reported to be at risk for development of cardiac valve regurgitation. Studies analyzing >500 prolactinoma patients receiving recommended doses of cabergoline (up to 2 mg weekly) have shown no evidence for an increased incidence of valvular disorders."

items.title: Clinical features and cardiovascular impact of acromegaly
items.content:
Acromegaly is INDOLENT — often undiagnosed for 10 years or more.
Acral/soft-tissue changes: frontal bossing, enlarged hands/feet, mandibular prognathism with widened tooth spacing, increased heel pad thickness, increased shoe/glove/ring size, coarse facial features, large nose, hyperhidrosis, deep voice, oily skin, arthropathy, kyphosis, carpal tunnel syndrome, proximal weakness, acanthosis nigricans, skin tags, and visceromegaly (cardiomegaly, macroglossia, goiter).
The most SIGNIFICANT clinical impact is CARDIOVASCULAR: cardiomyopathy with arrhythmias, left ventricular hypertrophy, decreased diastolic function, and hypertension occur in most untreated patients. Obstructive sleep apnea occurs in >60%. Diabetes mellitus develops in 25%. There is increased risk of colon polyps (up to one-third of patients) and colonic malignancy. Overall mortality is increased ~threefold, mainly from cardiovascular/cerebrovascular and respiratory disease.
keyPoints: Diagnosis is often delayed >10 years due to insidious onset.
Cardiovascular disease is the leading driver of excess mortality.
Increased colon polyp/cancer risk warrants colonoscopy screening.
Untreated GH excess shortens life expectancy by an average of 10 years.
items.reference: Acromegaly — Presentation and Diagnosis: "Unless GH levels are controlled, survival is reduced by an average of 10 years compared with an age-matched control population."

items.title: How acromegaly is diagnosed biochemically: IGF-1 screening and OGTT GH suppression
items.content:
GH is secreted in PULSES, so a single random GH level can be normal or elevated independent of overall disease severity and is NOT diagnostic.
IGF-1 (generated under GH's influence, stable over the day, interpreted against age-matched norms) is the SCREENING test of choice when clinical features raise suspicion.
CONFIRMATION uses an oral glucose tolerance test (75 g): GH is measured at 1-2 hours. Normal physiology suppresses GH to <0.4 µg/L (or <0.05 µg/L with ultrasensitive assays). FAILURE to suppress confirms acromegaly. About 20% of patients show a paradoxical GH RISE after glucose.
Additional work-up: PRL (elevated in ~25%, reflecting mixed somatolactotrope tumors); thyroid function and gonadal axes may be attenuated by mass effect. ACTH-reserve testing is deferred to after surgery, since perioperative glucocorticoid coverage is given regardless.
keyPoints: IGF-1 is the screening test — it reflects integrated GH secretion, unaffected by pulsatility.
OGTT with GH nadir >0.4 µg/L (or >0.05 by ultrasensitive assay) confirms acromegaly.
A single random GH level is NOT diagnostic.
PRL co-secretion occurs in ~25% (mixed somatolactotrope tumors).
items.reference: Acromegaly — Laboratory Investigation: "The diagnosis of acromegaly is confirmed by demonstrating the failure of GH suppression to <0.4 µg/L within 1–2 h of an oral glucose load (75 g)."

items.title: Treatment algorithm for acromegaly: surgery first, medical therapy for residual disease
items.content:
TRANSSPHENOIDAL SURGERY is first-line for most patients: remission rate ~70% for microadenomas but <50% for macroadenomas (which are more often invasive).
SOMATOSTATIN RECEPTOR LIGANDS (SRLs) are used adjuvantly: preoperative shrinkage of large invasive macroadenomas, immediate symptom relief, treatment in frail patients with high surgical morbidity, in patients who decline surgery, or when surgery fails to achieve biochemical control.
RADIATION is reserved for persistent/recurrent disease after surgery and medical therapy — its onset is slow (5-15 years), hypopituitarism rates are high, and it is relatively ineffective at normalizing IGF-1 on its own, often requiring an SRL "bridge" while awaiting effect.
keyPoints: Surgery is first-line; remission ~70% for microadenomas, <50% for macroadenomas.
SRLs: preoperative shrinkage, adjuvant for residual disease, or primary therapy if surgery is declined/contraindicated.
Radiation: last resort due to slow onset (5-15 years) and high rate of subsequent hypopituitarism.
items.reference: Acromegaly — Surgery: "Transsphenoidal surgical resection by an experienced surgeon is the preferred primary treatment for both microadenomas (remission rate ~70%) and macroadenomas (<50% in remission)."

items.title: How somatostatin receptor ligands control GH/IGF-1 hypersecretion in acromegaly
items.content:
SRLs (octreotide, lanreotide, pasireotide) act through SST2 and SST5 SOMATOSTATIN RECEPTORS expressed directly on the GH-secreting tumor cells, inhibiting GH release at its source.
• Octreotide LAR / lanreotide depot normalize GH and IGF-1 in ~50% of patients (lanreotide up to ~two-thirds with long-term use) and shrink the tumor in ~50%.
• Rapid relief of headache, soft-tissue swelling, sleep apnea, and cardiac symptoms often occurs within days to weeks — frequently BEFORE full biochemical normalization.
• Pasireotide (preferential SST5 binding) is used for tumors resistant to octreotide/lanreotide.
Side effects mostly reflect suppressed GI motility/secretion: transient nausea, diarrhea, malabsorption (improve within ~2 weeks); up to 30% develop gallstones/sludge; glucose intolerance from insulin suppression (more pronounced with pasireotide).
keyPoints: SRLs act directly on the tumor via SST2/SST5 — unlike pegvisomant, which acts peripherally.
Symptomatic improvement often precedes full biochemical (GH/IGF-1) normalization.
~30% develop gallstones/sludge; monitor glucose tolerance, especially with pasireotide.
items.reference: Acromegaly — Somatostatin Receptor Ligands: "SRLs exert their therapeutic effects through SST2 and SST5 receptor subtypes, both expressed by GH-secreting tumors."

items.title: Why GH levels remain elevated on pegvisomant even though IGF-1 normalizes
items.content:
Pegvisomant is a GH RECEPTOR ANTAGONIST — it binds peripheral GH receptors (e.g., on hepatocytes) and BLOCKS GH signal transduction, preventing IGF-1 generation. Crucially, it does NOT act on the pituitary tumor itself.
• Because the tumor's GH secretion is untouched (and reduced IGF-1 feedback at the pituitary may even increase GH output), circulating GH levels remain ELEVATED or rise further.
• Meanwhile IGF-1 — the downstream mediator of GH's growth-promoting and metabolic effects — falls to normal in ~70% of patients.
Clinical consequence: GH CANNOT be used to monitor pegvisomant efficacy. Response must be judged by IGF-1 levels (and clinical symptoms), and tumor size must be followed by MRI since pegvisomant does not shrink the tumor.
keyPoints: Pegvisomant blocks the GH receptor peripherally — it does not touch the pituitary tumor.
GH remains elevated; efficacy must be judged by IGF-1, not GH.
IGF-1 normalizes in ~70% of patients; tumor size must still be monitored by MRI.
items.reference: Acromegaly — GH Receptor Antagonist: "Pegvisomant antagonizes endogenous GH action by blocking peripheral GH binding to its receptor. Consequently, serum IGF-1 levels are suppressed, reducing the deleterious effects of excess endogenous GH... GH levels, however, remain elevated as the drug does not target the pituitary adenoma."

items.title: Cushing's disease in the differential diagnosis of Cushing's syndrome
items.content:
Pituitary CORTICOTROPE ADENOMAS ("Cushing's disease") cause 70% of ENDOGENOUS Cushing's syndrome. However, IATROGENIC glucocorticoid use remains the single most common overall cause of cushingoid features (exogenous, not endogenous).
ACTH-producing adenomas make up only 10-15% of all pituitary tumors and are usually tiny MICROADENOMAS (often <5 mm; about half are undetectable even by sensitive MRI) — because the systemic effects of cortisol excess lead to relatively early clinical recognition. Cushing's disease is 5-10 times more common in women.
These tumors retain PARTIAL suppressibility with high-dose glucocorticoids — the physiologic basis for dynamic testing (high-dose dexamethasone suppression) to distinguish a pituitary source from ectopic ACTH (which is typically resistant to suppression).
keyPoints: Cushing's disease = 70% of endogenous Cushing's syndrome; iatrogenic glucocorticoids = most common cause of cushingoid features overall.
ACTH-secreting adenomas are usually tiny microadenomas, often MRI-occult.
5-10x female predominance.
Retained high-dose glucocorticoid feedback suppressibility distinguishes pituitary from ectopic ACTH sources.
items.reference: Cushing's Disease — Etiology and Prevalence: "Pituitary corticotrope adenomas (Cushing's disease) account for 70% of patients with endogenous causes of Cushing's syndrome. However, it should be emphasized that iatrogenic hypercortisolism is the most common cause of cushingoid features."

items.title: Why severe hypokalemic alkalosis points toward ectopic, rather than pituitary, ACTH secretion
items.content:
Ectopic ACTH-secreting tumors typically produce MUCH higher ACTH levels (mean ~eightfold higher than pituitary Cushing's) and cause more abrupt, severe hypercortisolism.
• When cortisol production is massively elevated, it can overwhelm the renal mechanisms that normally inactivate cortisol before it reaches mineralocorticoid receptors.
• Cortisol then acts directly on renal mineralocorticoid receptors — driving sodium retention and potassium/hydrogen wasting → HYPOKALEMIC METABOLIC ALKALOSIS and hypertension.
• Pituitary Cushing's disease, with its lower-grade, more chronic hypercortisolism, much less often overwhelms this protective capacity.
Clinically: serum K+ <3.3 mmol/L is seen in ~70% of ectopic ACTH patients but in <10% of pituitary Cushing's disease patients — a useful bedside clue alongside rapid-onset hyperpigmentation and severe myopathy.
keyPoints: Ectopic ACTH secretion is typically more severe/abrupt than pituitary ACTH excess.
Massive cortisol excess can act directly on renal mineralocorticoid receptors → hypokalemic alkalosis.
Serum K+ <3.3 mmol/L: ~70% with ectopic ACTH vs <10% with pituitary Cushing's disease.
items.reference: Cushing's Disease — Presentation and Diagnosis: "Serum potassium levels <3.3 mmol/L are evident in ~70% of patients with ectopic ACTH secretion but are seen in <10% of patients with pituitary-dependent Cushing's syndrome."

items.title: How bilateral inferior petrosal sinus sampling localizes the source of ACTH excess
items.content:
Used when ACTH-DEPENDENT Cushing's syndrome is biochemically confirmed but MRI does NOT clearly show a pituitary adenoma (common, since many corticotrope microadenomas are <2 mm and MRI-occult) and the source (pituitary vs. ectopic) is unclear.
Catheters are placed in both inferior petrosal sinuses, with ACTH sampled at baseline and 2, 5, and 10 minutes after IV CRH (1 µg/kg):
• A PITUITARY source produces a steep CENTRAL-to-PERIPHERAL ACTH gradient — the tumor's venous effluent is concentrated locally before diluting into the systemic circulation. A baseline central:peripheral ratio >2, rising to >3 after CRH, confirms a pituitary source.
• An ECTOPIC source shows no such gradient, since ACTH enters the circulation more uniformly.
Sensitivity is >95%, but the procedure is technically demanding, with rare (~0.05%) neurovascular complications, and is AVOIDED if a clear adenoma is already visible on MRI or in patients with hypertension/cerebrovascular disease.
keyPoints: Indicated when ACTH-dependent Cushing's is confirmed but MRI doesn't clearly localize a pituitary adenoma.
Central:peripheral ACTH ratio >2 (baseline) or >3 (post-CRH) indicates a pituitary source.
Sensitivity >95%; rare but real (~0.05%) neurovascular risk; not performed if MRI already shows a clear adenoma.
items.reference: Inferior Petrosal Venous Sampling: "An increased central:peripheral ACTH ratio (>2) before and a peak central:peripheral ACTH ratio >3 after CRH injection confirm the presence of a pituitary ACTH-secreting adenoma. The sensitivity of this test is >95%, with very rare false-positive results."

items.title: Medical therapy options for Cushing's disease when surgery fails or is not possible
items.content:
Three broad categories of medical therapy:
• PITUITARY-DIRECTED: pasireotide LAR (SST5-preferential SRL) normalizes 24-h UFC in ~20% and shrinks the tumor in up to 40%, but causes hyperglycemia/new-onset diabetes in up to 70% of patients (suppressed insulin/incretin secretion).
• ADRENAL STEROIDOGENESIS INHIBITORS: osilodrostat (11β-hydroxylase inhibitor; normalizes UFC in 86%, but risks adrenal insufficiency, hypokalemia/hypertension from precursor accumulation, and QTc prolongation); ketoconazole/levoketoconazole (P450 inhibitors; hepatotoxicity, gynecomastia); metyrapone (11β-hydroxylase inhibitor; normalizes cortisol in up to 75%); mitotane (adrenolytic, slow-onset).
• GLUCOCORTICOID RECEPTOR ANTAGONIST: mifepristone blocks cortisol's PERIPHERAL action without lowering ACTH or cortisol — so these levels remain elevated and CANNOT be used to titrate the dose; approved specifically for hyperglycemia in Cushing's syndrome; risks include hypoadrenalism (paradoxical, since the hormone is blocked not reduced), hypokalemia, and endometrial hyperplasia.
keyPoints: Steroidogenesis inhibitors (osilodrostat, metyrapone, ketoconazole, mitotane) lower cortisol directly — monitor for iatrogenic adrenal insufficiency.
Mifepristone blocks the receptor, not production — ACTH/cortisol stay high, so clinical response (not labs) guides dosing.
Pasireotide targets the tumor itself but causes diabetes in up to 70% of patients.
items.reference: Cushing's Disease — Treatment: "Mifepristone (300–1200 mg/d), a glucocorticoid receptor antagonist, blocks peripheral cortisol action and is approved to treat hyperglycemia in Cushing's disease. Because the drug does not target the pituitary tumor, both ACTH and cortisol levels remain elevated, thus obviating a reliable circulating biomarker."

items.title: Why bilateral adrenalectomy for Cushing's disease can trigger Nelson's syndrome
items.content:
Bilateral adrenalectomy removes the SOURCE of cortisol — and with it, the NEGATIVE FEEDBACK that cortisol normally exerts on a residual ACTH-secreting corticotrope adenoma — while leaving the tumor tissue itself in place.
• Loss of cortisol feedback removes the brake on corticotrope cell proliferation and ACTH/POMC-derived peptide secretion (including melanocyte-stimulating peptides).
• The result is RAPID pituitary tumor enlargement and marked HYPERPIGMENTATION from the high ACTH/MSH-related peptide levels — Nelson's syndrome.
Prophylactic pituitary RADIATION at the time of adrenalectomy can reduce this risk by limiting the residual tumor's growth potential.
keyPoints: Adrenalectomy removes cortisol's negative feedback on the residual corticotrope adenoma.
Unchecked tumor growth + high ACTH/MSH-related peptides → rapid enlargement and hyperpigmentation (Nelson's syndrome).
Prophylactic pituitary radiotherapy can be used to prevent Nelson's syndrome.
items.reference: Cushing's Disease — Treatment: "Adrenalectomy in the setting of residual corticotrope adenoma tissue predisposes to the development of Nelson's syndrome, a disorder characterized by rapid pituitary tumor enlargement and increased pigmentation secondary to high ACTH levels."

items.title: Clinically nonfunctioning pituitary adenomas: presentation, hormones, and management
items.content:
Nonfunctioning adenomas are the MOST COMMON type of pituitary adenoma overall, but because they cause no hormone-excess syndrome, they are usually MACROADENOMAS by the time of diagnosis — presenting with mass effects (visual field loss) or as incidentalomas.
Most arise from GONADOTROPE or null cells, secreting small amounts of intact FSH (rarely LH), free α-subunit, and FSHβ/LHβ subunits — usually insufficient to cause a recognizable syndrome (rare large tumors can cause an ovarian-hyperstimulation-like picture). Stalk/gland compression causes mild hyperprolactinemia (usually PRL <100 µg/L) and hypogonadism with LOW testosterone despite normal/high LH (reduced LH bioactivity/pulsatility).
Management: small, asymptomatic microadenomas → MRI surveillance (repeat at 3 years, then as needed); macroadenomas → transsphenoidal surgery (vision improves in 70% of those with preoperative defects); adjuvant radiotherapy if substantial residual tumor remains; ~15% recur within 5-6 years post-resection.
keyPoints: Most common pituitary adenoma type overall; usually presents as a macroadenoma due to lack of a hormonal syndrome.
Mild hyperprolactinemia (usually <100 µg/L) from stalk compression should not be mistaken for a prolactinoma.
Small asymptomatic microadenomas: MRI surveillance; macroadenomas: surgery, with vision improvement in ~70%.
items.reference: Nonfunctioning and Gonadotropin-Producing Pituitary Adenomas — Treatment: "As the probability of nonfunctioning microadenoma growth is very low, asymptomatic small nonfunctioning microadenomas with no threat to vision may be followed with an MRI after 3 years, with serial MRI and visual field testing thereafter as needed."

items.title: Why PRL suppression by a dopamine agonist does not prove a pituitary mass is a prolactinoma
items.content:
In a nonfunctioning adenoma, elevated PRL arises because STALK COMPRESSION reduces hypothalamic dopamine delivery to otherwise NORMAL lactotropes — the lactotropes themselves are not neoplastic.
• A dopamine agonist can still lower PRL toward normal by directly stimulating D2 receptors on these normal lactotropes.
• But the dopamine agonist has NO effect on the nonfunctioning tumor mass itself, which does not depend on dopamine signaling for its growth — so the TUMOR DOES NOT SHRINK.
Clinical consequence: normalization of PRL with a dopamine agonist does NOT confirm a diagnosis of prolactinoma. Only a true prolactinoma shows BOTH biochemical (PRL) AND structural (tumor size) response to dopamine agonist therapy.
keyPoints: Stalk-compression hyperprolactinemia arises from normal lactotropes losing dopamine inhibition — not from a PRL-secreting tumor.
Dopamine agonists can normalize PRL without shrinking a nonfunctioning adenoma.
True prolactinomas respond with BOTH PRL normalization AND tumor shrinkage.
items.reference: Nonfunctioning and Gonadotropin-Producing Pituitary Adenomas — Presentation and Diagnosis: "It is important to remember that hyperprolactinemia caused secondarily by the mass effects of nonlactotrope lesions is also corrected by treatment with dopamine agonists despite failure to shrink the underlying mass. Consequently, PRL suppression by dopamine agonists does not necessarily indicate that the underlying lesion is a prolactinoma."

items.title: TSH-secreting pituitary adenomas: recognizing 'inappropriate' TSH and treatment approach
items.content:
TSH-secreting adenomas are RARE but often large, locally invasive macroadenomas, presenting with GOITER and HYPERTHYROIDISM from chronic TSH overproduction.
DIAGNOSIS: elevated free T4 with INAPPROPRIATELY NORMAL or HIGH TSH (in primary hyperthyroidism, TSH would be suppressed) plus a pituitary mass on MRI; elevated free α-subunit supports the diagnosis. Must exclude resistance to thyroid hormone (RTH, an autosomal-dominant THRβ mutation) and dysalbuminemic hyperthyroxinemia (a familial albumin-binding protein mutation, distinguished by NORMAL free hormone levels and normal TSH).
TREATMENT: transsphenoidal surgery first-line (normalizes thyroid hormone in ~two-thirds, though complete resection is uncommon given tumor size/invasiveness). SRLs normalize TSH/α-subunit, shrink the tumor in ~50%, and improve visual fields in 75% — but because SRLs markedly suppress TSH, concomitant thyroid hormone replacement is often needed.
keyPoints: 'Inappropriate' TSH = elevated/normal TSH despite elevated free T4 — think pituitary TSH adenoma or thyroid hormone resistance.
Differentiate from resistance to thyroid hormone (THRβ mutation) and dysalbuminemic hyperthyroxinemia (normal free hormone levels).
SRLs are effective adjuncts but may require thyroid hormone replacement due to TSH suppression.
items.reference: TSH-Secreting Adenomas: "Diagnosis is based on demonstrating elevated serum free T4 levels, inappropriately normal or high TSH secretion, and MRI evidence of a pituitary adenoma."

items.title: Why certain pituitary adenoma subtypes are prone to aggressive growth and require Ki-67-guided surveillance
items.content:
True pituitary CARCINOMA (with documented extracranial metastases) is exceedingly rare — but a distinct subset of adenomas behaves AGGRESSIVELY without metastasizing:
• SILENT corticotrope and somatotrope tumors (those secreting ACTH/GH at levels too low to cause a clinical hypersecretion syndrome, yet retaining proliferative drive).
• PROLACTINOMAS in middle-aged MEN (consistent with the broader pattern that male prolactinomas tend to present larger and more invasive).
These tumors show high Ki-67 proliferation indices (>4%), local CNS invasion, and high recurrence rates. Because conventional therapy (repeat surgery, irradiation) often fails to control them, TEMOZOLOMIDE (an alkylating chemotherapy used in glioma) has been used, with VARIABLE responses — underscoring the need for integrated, multimodal long-term management.
keyPoints: 'Silent' corticotrope/somatotrope adenomas and male prolactinomas in middle age are most likely to behave aggressively.
Ki-67 >4% identifies aggressive biology, even though true pituitary carcinoma (with metastasis) is exceedingly rare.
Temozolomide is used for refractory aggressive adenomas, with variable responses.
items.reference: Aggressive Adenomas: "Silent corticotrope and somatotrope tumors, as well as prolactinomas occurring in middle-aged men, are particularly prone to aggressive growth and recurrence. Patients with these tumors usually require an integrated management approach including repeat surgeries and irradiation. Temozolomide has also been used with variable responses."

items.question: A 45-year-old woman undergoes visual field testing for a 2-cm pituitary macroadenoma with suprasellar extension abutting the optic chiasm. Which visual field defect is most classically associated with this lesion?
options: Bitemporal hemianopia, often worse in the superior quadrants
Monocular blindness in one eye only
items.explanation: Pituitary masses expand upward toward the chiasm from below, compressing the crossing nasal retinal fibers (which carry temporal field information) in the ventral chiasm first — producing bitemporal hemianopia, typically worse superiorly. Homonymous hemianopia instead suggests postchiasmal compression.
items.reference: Ophthalmologic Evaluation: "Bitemporal hemianopia, often more pronounced superiorly, is observed classically. It occurs because nasal ganglion cell fibers, which cross in the optic chiasm, are especially vulnerable to compression of the ventral optic chiasm."

items.question: A 2.5-cm nonfunctioning pituitary macroadenoma is found on MRI in a man being evaluated for headaches. Serum prolactin is 65 µg/L. What is the most appropriate interpretation?
options: This PRL level confirms a coexisting prolactinoma requiring cabergoline
The PRL elevation is most consistent with pituitary stalk compression reducing dopamine delivery, not a true prolactinoma
This PRL level is normal and requires no explanation
Pregnancy must be excluded as the cause
items.explanation: Stalk compression by a nonfunctioning mass disrupts dopamine delivery, causing mild-to-moderate hyperprolactinemia (typically 30-100 µg/L). A true prolactinoma of this size would typically produce PRL well above 200-250 µg/L, correlating with tumor mass.
items.reference: Hyperprolactinemia: "Pituitary masses, including clinically nonfunctioning pituitary tumors, may compress the pituitary stalk to cause hyperprolactinemia."

items.question: A 6-mm pituitary lesion is found incidentally on MRI performed for headache evaluation. Hormonal screening (PRL, IGF-1, UFC, thyroid function, gonadotropins) is normal. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Serial MRI surveillance, performed annually and then less often if stable, with repeat hormone screening as needed
items.explanation: Small, non-hypersecreting incidentalomas can be safely monitored with serial MRI (annual, then less frequent if stable). Surgery is reserved for larger macroadenomas at risk of invasion/mass effect or any tumor with hormone hypersecretion.
items.reference: Magnetic Resonance Imaging: "In the absence of hormone hypersecretion, these small intrasellar lesions can be monitored safely with MRI, which is performed annually and then less often if there is no evidence of further growth."

items.question: An 8-year-old presents with headache, vomiting, papilledema, and bitemporal hemianopia. MRI shows a suprasellar cystic mass with partial calcification. Which diagnosis is most likely?
items.explanation: Craniopharyngiomas are benign, often partially calcified, suprasellar cystic masses arising from Rathke's pouch remnants. More than half of patients present before age 20 with signs of increased intracranial pressure (headache, vomiting, papilledema) plus visual field deficits.
items.reference: Sellar Masses: "Craniopharyngiomas are benign, suprasellar cystic masses that present with headaches, visual field deficits, and variable degrees of hypopituitarism... Many are partially calcified, exhibiting a characteristic appearance on skull x-ray and CT images."

items.question: A patient with a recurrent papillary craniopharyngioma not amenable to further safe resection is found to harbor a BRAF V600E mutation. Which targeted therapy approach has shown long-term growth responses in such patients?
options: A BRAF inhibitor (dabrafenib or vemurafenib), alone or combined with a MEK inhibitor (trametinib or cobimetinib)
items.explanation: Papillary craniopharyngiomas frequently harbor activating BRAF V600E mutations. BRAF inhibitors, alone or combined with MEK inhibitors, have produced long-term growth responses, offering a medical option when surgery alone is unlikely to be curative.
items.reference: Sellar Masses: "As some craniopharyngiomas (particularly papillary) are associated with activated BRAF V600E mutations, use of BRAF inhibitors (dabrafenib or vemurafenib) either alone or in combination with MEK inhibitors (trametinib or cobimetinib) has resulted in long-term growth responses in some patients."

items.question: A 58-year-old woman with metastatic breast cancer develops polyuria and polydipsia with dilute urine. MRI shows thickening of the pituitary stalk and posterior pituitary. What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: AVP deficiency (central diabetes insipidus) from a metastatic deposit
Syndrome of inappropriate antidiuretic hormone secretion
items.explanation: Hematogenous metastases (breast cancer accounts for about half of pituitary metastases) seed almost exclusively the posterior pituitary, where they disrupt AVP storage/release — AVP deficiency (central DI) with polyuria, polydipsia, and dilute urine is often the presenting feature.
items.reference: Sellar Masses: "Bloodborne metastatic deposits are found almost exclusively in the posterior pituitary. Accordingly, AVP-D can be a presenting feature of lung, gastrointestinal, breast, and other pituitary metastases."

items.question: A 30-year-old man presents with hypercalcemia from a parathyroid adenoma, a pancreatic gastrinoma, and a pituitary mass with hyperprolactinemia. Which underlying syndrome and gene is most likely?
items.explanation: MEN1 (autosomal dominant, inactivating germline MENIN mutation on chromosome 11q13) predisposes to parathyroid, pancreatic islet, and pituitary adenomas. About half of MEN1 patients develop prolactinomas.
items.reference: Genetic Syndromes Associated with Pituitary Tumors: "MEN 1 is caused by inactivating germline mutations in MENIN, a constitutively expressed tumor-suppressor gene located on chromosome 11q13... About half of affected patients develop prolactinomas; acromegaly and Cushing's disease are less commonly encountered."

items.question: Several members of a family present with acromegaly or gigantism at a young age, without other endocrine tumors. Which germline mutation is most likely responsible?
items.explanation: A subset of families with familial acromegaly/gigantism harbor germline mutations in AIP, which encodes the aryl hydrocarbon receptor interacting protein.
items.reference: Genetic Syndromes Associated with Pituitary Tumors: "A subset of families with a predisposition for familial pituitary tumors, especially acromegaly, has been found to harbor germline mutations in the AIP gene, which encodes the aryl hydrocarbon receptor interacting protein."

items.question: A 35-year-old man has a 3-cm pituitary macroadenoma with visual field defects, but his measured PRL is only 150 µg/L — lower than expected for a tumor this size. What is the best next step?
options: Accept the result and diagnose a nonfunctioning adenoma
Repeat PRL measurement with serial sample dilution to exclude a 'hook effect' assay artifact
Start bromocriptine immediately without further testing
No further PRL testing is needed since MRI already shows a mass
items.explanation: At extremely high true PRL concentrations (>1000 µg/L), the immunoassay can become saturated and report a falsely LOW value (the 'hook effect'). A large macroadenoma with only modestly elevated PRL should raise suspicion for this artifact; serial dilution reveals the true value.
items.reference: Laboratory Investigation: "In patients with markedly elevated PRL levels (>1000 µg/L), reported results may be falsely lowered because of assay artifacts; sample dilution is required to measure these high values accurately."

items.question: A 28-year-old woman has an incidentally found 4-mm pituitary lesion with PRL of 45 µg/L, regular menses, and no desire for pregnancy. What is the most appropriate management?
options: Observation with periodic PRL measurement and MRI, since microadenomas rarely progress
items.explanation: Asymptomatic microprolactinomas rarely progress to macroadenomas (~5% in the long term). If the patient is asymptomatic and fertility is not desired, no treatment is needed — regular serial PRL and MRI monitoring suffices.
items.reference: Prolactinoma — Treatment: "Because microadenomas rarely progress to become macroadenomas, no treatment may be needed if patients are asymptomatic and fertility is not desired; these patients should be monitored by regular serial PRL measurements and MRI scans."

items.question: A woman with a microprolactinoma on cabergoline now wishes to conceive. What is the most appropriate management?
options: Switch to bromocriptine, then discontinue it once pregnancy is confirmed
Proceed directly to transsphenoidal surgery before attempting conception
items.explanation: Bromocriptine is short-acting with extensive safety data in pregnancy and is preferred when conception is desired; it is discontinued once pregnancy is confirmed. Cabergoline's long half-life and high D2 affinity make it less suitable when fertility is the immediate goal.
items.reference: Pregnancy: "Because cabergoline is long-acting with a high D2-receptor affinity, it is not recommended for use in women when fertility is desired."

items.question: A man with a macroprolactinoma has persistent PRL elevation (600 µg/L) despite maximally tolerated cabergoline. What is the most likely mechanism underlying this resistance?
options: Decreased D2 dopamine receptor numbers or a postreceptor signaling defect in the tumor
items.explanation: About 20% of prolactinoma patients, more often men, are resistant to dopamine agonist treatment due to decreased D2 receptor numbers or a postreceptor defect.
items.reference: Prolactinoma — Treatment: "About 20% of patients (especially males) are resistant to dopaminergic treatment; these adenomas may exhibit decreased D2 dopamine receptor numbers or a postreceptor defect."

items.question: A 50-year-old man presents with new-onset diabetes, progressively enlarging hands, feet, and jaw, and carpal tunnel syndrome. What is the best initial screening test for acromegaly?
options: A single random serum GH level
items.explanation: GH secretion is pulsatile, making a random GH level unreliable. Age-matched IGF-1 reflects integrated GH secretion and is the appropriate screening test when clinical features suggest acromegaly.
items.reference: Acromegaly — Laboratory Investigation: "Age-matched serum IGF-1 levels are elevated in acromegaly. Consequently, an IGF-1 level provides a useful laboratory screening measure when clinical features raise the possibility of acromegaly."

items.question: Following an elevated age-matched IGF-1 in a patient with suspected acromegaly, which test confirms the diagnosis?
options: Oral glucose tolerance test with GH measured at 1-2 hours; failure to suppress GH to <0.4 µg/L confirms acromegaly
items.explanation: The diagnosis of acromegaly is confirmed by failure of GH to suppress to <0.4 µg/L (or <0.05 µg/L with ultrasensitive assays) within 1-2 hours of a 75-g oral glucose load.
items.reference: Acromegaly — Laboratory Investigation: "The diagnosis of acromegaly is confirmed by demonstrating the failure of GH suppression to <0.4 µg/L within 1–2 h of an oral glucose load (75 g)."

items.question: A 55-year-old man is newly diagnosed with acromegaly. Which additional screening is specifically indicated because of this diagnosis?
options: Colonoscopy, given the increased risk of colon polyps and colorectal malignancy
No additional cancer screening is indicated
items.explanation: Acromegaly is associated with an increased risk of colon polyps (diagnosed in up to one-third of patients) and mortality from colorectal malignancy, warranting colonoscopic screening.
items.reference: Acromegaly — Presentation and Diagnosis: "Acromegaly is associated with an increased risk of colon polyps and mortality from colonic malignancy; polyps are diagnosed in up to one-third of patients."

items.question: A 45-year-old woman is diagnosed with a GH-secreting macroadenoma causing significant suprasellar extension and a visual field deficit. What is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
options: Observation with repeat MRI in 6 months
items.explanation: Transsphenoidal surgery is the preferred initial treatment for GH-secreting adenomas, including macroadenomas, particularly when there is mass effect (visual field deficit) requiring decompression.
items.reference: Acromegaly — Surgery: "Transsphenoidal surgical resection by an experienced surgeon is the preferred primary treatment for both microadenomas (remission rate ~70%) and macroadenomas (<50% in remission)."

items.question: After subtotal resection of a GH-secreting macroadenoma, IGF-1 remains elevated. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Adjuvant somatostatin receptor ligand therapy (octreotide or lanreotide LAR)
Immediate repeat surgery before any medical therapy
Pegvisomant as the universal first-choice adjuvant agent
Observation only, since IGF-1 normalizes spontaneously over time
items.explanation: SRLs are the preferred adjuvant medical therapy for residual GH hypersecretion after surgery, normalizing GH/IGF-1 in about half of patients and providing rapid symptomatic relief while also reducing tumor size in some.
items.reference: Acromegaly — Treatment: "SRLs are used as adjuvant treatment for preoperative shrinkage of large invasive macroadenomas, immediate relief of debilitating symptoms, and reduction of GH hypersecretion; in frail patients experiencing morbidity; and in patients who decline surgery or when surgery fails to achieve biochemical control."

items.question: A patient on pegvisomant for resistant acromegaly reports symptomatic improvement and has a normalized IGF-1, but the serum GH level remains elevated. How should this be interpreted?
options: Treatment failure — GH should normalize with effective therapy
Expected; pegvisomant blocks peripheral GH action without lowering pituitary GH secretion, so efficacy is judged by IGF-1, not GH
It indicates an urgent need to add a dopamine agonist
It indicates tumor growth and mandates immediate repeat surgery
items.explanation: Pegvisomant is a GH receptor antagonist acting peripherally; it does not target the pituitary tumor, so GH levels remain elevated even with effective treatment. IGF-1 (and clinical response) is used to monitor efficacy; tumor size is followed separately by MRI.
items.reference: Acromegaly — GH Receptor Antagonist: "Pegvisomant antagonizes endogenous GH action by blocking peripheral GH binding to its receptor. Consequently, serum IGF-1 levels are suppressed... GH levels, however, remain elevated as the drug does not target the pituitary adenoma."

items.question: A 35-year-old woman has central obesity, purple striae, and proximal muscle weakness. An MRI performed for an unrelated reason previously showed a 4-mm pituitary lesion. What is the most appropriate first step?
options: Proceed directly to transsphenoidal surgery based on the imaging finding
Biochemically confirm hypercortisolism (e.g., 24-h urinary free cortisol or overnight dexamethasone suppression test) before further evaluation
Start ketoconazole empirically without confirming the diagnosis
items.explanation: Given the high prevalence of incidental pituitary lesions, hypercortisolism must be biochemically confirmed before pursuing localization studies, imaging-based decisions, or therapy.
items.reference: Cushing's Disease — Laboratory Investigation: "Measurement of 24-h UFC is a precise and cost-effective screening test. Alternatively, the failure to suppress plasma cortisol after an overnight 1-mg dexamethasone suppression test can be used to identify patients with hypercortisolism."

items.question: A patient with ACTH-dependent Cushing's syndrome has a serum potassium of 2.8 mmol/L, rapid-onset hyperpigmentation, and severe proximal myopathy. Which source of ACTH excess is most likely?
items.explanation: Rapid onset of hypercortisolism features with hyperpigmentation and severe myopathy, along with marked hypokalemic alkalosis (K <3.3 mmol/L in ~70% of cases), suggests an ectopic ACTH-secreting tumor rather than a pituitary corticotrope adenoma.
items.reference: Cushing's Disease — Presentation and Diagnosis: "Rapid development of features of hypercortisolism associated with skin hyperpigmentation and severe myopathy suggests an ectopic tumor source of ACTH. Hypertension, hypokalemic alkalosis, glucose intolerance, and edema are also more pronounced in these patients."

items.question: A patient has confirmed ACTH-dependent Cushing's syndrome, but pituitary MRI shows no definite adenoma. Which test best localizes the source of ACTH?
options: Bilateral inferior petrosal sinus sampling with CRH stimulation
Repeat pituitary MRI in 6 months with no further testing
items.explanation: When ACTH-dependent Cushing's is confirmed but MRI does not show a clear adenoma, bilateral inferior petrosal sinus sampling before and after CRH (sensitivity >95%) distinguishes a pituitary from an ectopic ACTH source via the central:peripheral ACTH gradient.
items.reference: Inferior Petrosal Venous Sampling: "An increased central:peripheral ACTH ratio (>2) before and a peak central:peripheral ACTH ratio >3 after CRH injection confirm the presence of a pituitary ACTH-secreting adenoma. The sensitivity of this test is >95%, with very rare false-positive results."

items.question: Two days after successful transsphenoidal resection of an ACTH-secreting microadenoma, a patient develops fatigue, hypotension, and a low morning cortisol. What is the best interpretation and management?
options: Expected postoperative ACTH deficiency from a chronically suppressed hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis, requiring glucocorticoid replacement that may be needed for up to 12 months
Surgical failure — repeat surgery should be performed immediately
No treatment needed; the findings will resolve within hours
items.explanation: After successful resection of an ACTH-secreting adenoma, the chronically suppressed normal corticotropes take time to recover. A postoperative period of symptomatic ACTH deficiency (lasting up to 12 months) is expected — and is actually a favorable sign of cure — requiring glucocorticoid replacement and steroid withdrawal management.
items.reference: Cushing's Disease — Treatment: "After successful tumor resection, most patients experience a postoperative period of symptomatic ACTH deficiency that may last up to 12 months. This usually requires low-dose cortisol replacement, as patients experience steroid withdrawal symptoms and have a suppressed hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis."

items.question: A patient with persistent Cushing's disease started on pasireotide LAR develops new-onset hyperglycemia. What is the primary mechanism?
options: Direct toxic effect on pancreatic beta cells
Suppression of insulin and incretin secretion via somatostatin receptors
Increased cortisol production caused by the drug
Increased peripheral insulin sensitivity leading to rebound hyperglycemia
items.explanation: Pasireotide binds somatostatin receptors on pancreatic islet cells, suppressing both insulin and incretin secretion. This explains why hyperglycemia and new-onset diabetes develop in up to 70% of patients treated with pasireotide.
items.reference: Cushing's Disease — Treatment: "Notably, hyperglycemia and new-onset diabetes develop in up to 70% of patients, likely due to suppressed pancreatic secretion of insulin and incretins."

items.question: A patient on osilodrostat for Cushing's disease should be monitored for which of the following adverse effects?
options: Adrenal insufficiency, hypokalemia, and hypertension from accumulation of adrenal steroid precursors
Hyperkalemia and bradycardia as the primary concerns
Hair loss as the dose-limiting toxicity
items.explanation: Osilodrostat, an 11β-hydroxylase inhibitor, can cause adrenal insufficiency from excessive cortisol blockade, while accumulation of upstream steroid precursors can cause hypokalemia and hypertension; QTc prolongation is also reported.
items.reference: Cushing's Disease — Treatment: "Patients should be closely monitored for development of hypocortisolism and adrenal insufficiency. Elevated adrenal hormone precursors may lead to hypokalemia and hypertension."

items.question: A patient with Cushing's syndrome-related hyperglycemia is started on mifepristone. Because ACTH and cortisol levels remain elevated during treatment, how should the response to therapy be monitored?
options: By clinical parameters such as glucose control and blood pressure, not by ACTH or cortisol levels
By serial 24-h urinary free cortisol levels
items.explanation: Mifepristone blocks the glucocorticoid receptor peripherally without lowering ACTH or cortisol, so these biomarkers cannot be used to titrate therapy. Clinical response (e.g., glycemic control, blood pressure) guides dosing.
items.reference: Cushing's Disease — Treatment: "Mifepristone... blocks peripheral cortisol action and is approved to treat hyperglycemia in Cushing's disease. Because the drug does not target the pituitary tumor, both ACTH and cortisol levels remain elevated, thus obviating a reliable circulating biomarker."

items.question: A 60-year-old man has a 2.5-cm nonfunctioning pituitary macroadenoma causing bitemporal hemianopia. What is the most appropriate management?
items.explanation: For macroadenomas causing compressive mass effects such as visual field defects, transsphenoidal surgery is indicated; vision improves in about 70% of patients with preoperative visual field defects. Nonfunctioning adenomas respond poorly to dopamine agonists and SRLs.
items.reference: Nonfunctioning and Gonadotropin-Producing Pituitary Adenomas — Treatment: "for macroadenomas, transsphenoidal surgery is indicated to reduce tumor size and relieve compressive mass effects... vision improves in 70% of patients with preoperative visual field defects."

items.question: A 55-year-old man has a pituitary mass with mildly elevated FSH (greater than LH) and a low testosterone level. How should this hormone pattern be interpreted?
options: It is diagnostic of primary gonadal failure and excludes a pituitary tumor
It is consistent with a gonadotrope adenoma, but overlaps with primary gonadal failure and aging, so diagnosis often requires immunohistochemistry of resected tissue
It excludes the possibility of a pituitary tumor
It is diagnostic of Klinefelter syndrome
items.explanation: Mildly elevated gonadotropins (FSH > LH) with low testosterone can be seen with gonadotrope adenomas, primary gonadal failure, and aging — the pattern alone is insufficient for diagnosis. Confirmation typically requires immunohistochemical analysis of resected tumor tissue.
items.reference: Nonfunctioning and Gonadotropin-Producing Pituitary Adenomas — Laboratory Investigation: "Because this pattern of hormone test results is also seen in primary gonadal failure and, to some extent, with aging... the finding of increased gonadotropins alone is insufficient for the diagnosis of a gonadotropin-secreting tumor."

items.question: A 40-year-old woman has a goiter, tachycardia, and an elevated free T4, but her TSH is normal rather than suppressed. MRI shows a pituitary mass. What is the most likely diagnosis?
items.explanation: Elevated free T4 with an inappropriately normal (or high) TSH, in the presence of a pituitary mass, is characteristic of a TSH-secreting adenoma — the TSH that should be suppressed by feedback from elevated thyroid hormone is not.
items.reference: TSH-Secreting Adenomas: "Diagnosis is based on demonstrating elevated serum free T4 levels, inappropriately normal or high TSH secretion, and MRI evidence of a pituitary adenoma."

items.question: In the patient from the previous question, free α-subunit is elevated and the pituitary mass is confirmed. Which feature helps distinguish a TSH-secreting adenoma from resistance to thyroid hormone (RTH)?
options: The presence of a pituitary mass with elevated free α-subunit favors a TSH-secreting adenoma; RTH results from a thyroid hormone receptor β mutation without a pituitary mass
Both conditions are indistinguishable and managed identically
RTH always presents with suppressed TSH
A TSH-secreting adenoma always presents with hypothyroidism
items.explanation: Resistance to thyroid hormone is an autosomal dominant disorder caused by THRβ mutations and does not produce a pituitary mass or elevated free α-subunit. A pituitary mass with elevated free α-subunit and β-subunit levels favors a TSH-secreting adenoma.
items.reference: TSH-Secreting Adenomas: "It is important to exclude other causes of inappropriate TSH secretion, such as resistance to thyroid hormone, an autosomal dominant disorder caused by mutations in the thyroid hormone β receptor... The presence of a pituitary mass and elevated β subunit levels are suggestive of a TSH-secreting tumor."

items.question: A middle-aged man has a recurrent, locally invasive prolactinoma despite surgery and maximal dopamine agonist therapy, with a Ki-67 index of 6%. Which therapy may be considered for this aggressive adenoma?
items.explanation: Aggressive adenomas (high Ki-67, often silent corticotrope/somatotrope tumors or prolactinomas in middle-aged men) that fail conventional therapy may be treated with temozolomide, an alkylating chemotherapy, with variable responses.
items.reference: Aggressive Adenomas: "Silent corticotrope and somatotrope tumors, as well as prolactinomas occurring in middle-aged men, are particularly prone to aggressive growth and recurrence... Temozolomide has also been used with variable responses."

items.statement: Bitemporal hemianopia caused by a pituitary adenoma is typically worse in the inferior visual field quadrants than the superior quadrants.
items.explanation: The opposite is true. Because the crossing nasal fibers occupy the ventral (inferior) chiasm, an expanding pituitary mass compresses them first, producing bitemporal hemianopia that is classically worse in the SUPERIOR quadrants.
items.reference: Ophthalmologic Evaluation: "Bitemporal hemianopia, often more pronounced superiorly, is observed classically. It occurs because nasal ganglion cell fibers, which cross in the optic chiasm, are especially vulnerable to compression of the ventral optic chiasm."

items.statement: Pituitary stalk compression causes hyperprolactinemia because loss of dopamine removes a tonic inhibitory signal, whereas loss of other hypothalamic releasing hormones causes deficiency of their target pituitary hormones.
items.explanation: Dopamine is unique among hypothalamic factors in being inhibitory for prolactin. Its loss disinhibits lactotropes (raising PRL), while loss of GnRH, GHRH, TRH, and CRH lowers their respective target hormones.
items.reference: Local Mass Effects: "Pituitary stalk compression by a hormonally active or inactive intrasellar mass may compress the portal vessels, disrupting pituitary access to hypothalamic hormones and dopamine; this results in early hyperprolactinemia and later concurrent loss of other pituitary hormones."

items.statement: All pituitary incidentalomas, regardless of size or hormone status, require immediate surgical resection.
items.explanation: Small incidentalomas without hormone hypersecretion can be safely monitored with serial MRI. Surgery is considered for larger incidental macroadenomas (about one-third become invasive/symptomatic) or any tumor with hormone hypersecretion.
items.reference: Magnetic Resonance Imaging: "In the absence of hormone hypersecretion, these small intrasellar lesions can be monitored safely with MRI, which is performed annually and then less often if there is no evidence of further growth. Resection should be considered for incidentally discovered larger macroadenomas, because about one-third become invasive or cause local pressure effects."

items.statement: CT scanning is reserved for assessing bony erosion of the sella or detecting calcification, while gadolinium-enhanced MRI is the primary modality for characterizing sellar masses.
items.explanation: MRI provides superior soft-tissue contrast for the pituitary, stalk, and chiasm. CT's role is limited to defining bony involvement or calcification (e.g., in craniopharyngioma).
items.reference: Magnetic Resonance Imaging: "Computed tomography (CT) scan is reserved to define the extent of bony erosion or the presence of calcification."

items.statement: Pituitary metastases occur almost exclusively in the posterior pituitary because of its direct arterial blood supply, and AVP deficiency (central diabetes insipidus) can be the presenting feature.
items.explanation: Hematogenous metastatic spread preferentially involves the posterior pituitary, which has a direct hypophyseal arterial supply, often disrupting AVP storage/release before causing anterior pituitary failure.
items.reference: Sellar Masses: "Bloodborne metastatic deposits are found almost exclusively in the posterior pituitary. Accordingly, AVP-D can be a presenting feature of lung, gastrointestinal, breast, and other pituitary metastases."

items.statement: GnRH-secreting hypothalamic hamartomas causing central precocious puberty are treated with pulsatile GnRH administration to restore normal pubertal timing.
items.explanation: Treatment uses long-acting (continuous-exposure) GnRH ANALOGUES, which paradoxically desensitize pituitary gonadotropes and SUPPRESS gonadotropin secretion, halting premature puberty — the opposite of pulsatile administration.
items.reference: Sellar Masses: "Medical treatment of GnRH-producing hamartomas with long-acting GnRH analogues effectively suppresses gonadotropin secretion and controls premature pubertal development."

items.statement: Approximately 35% of GH-secreting pituitary adenomas harbor somatic Gα mutations that impair the intrinsic GTPase 'off switch,' leading to constitutive elevation of cyclic AMP.
items.explanation: These 'gsp' mutations lock Gα in its active state, sustaining cAMP signaling, Pit-1 induction, and CREB activation — driving autonomous somatotrope proliferation and GH hypersecretion.
items.reference: Pathogenesis: "A subset (~35%) of GH-secreting pituitary tumors contains sporadic mutations in Gα. These mutations attenuate intrinsic GTPase activity, resulting in constitutive elevation of cyclic AMP, Pit-1 induction, and activation of cyclic AMP response element binding protein (CREB), thereby promoting somatotrope cell proliferation and GH secretion."

items.statement: The activating Gα mutation responsible for McCune-Albright syndrome is present in the germline and inherited in an autosomal dominant pattern.
items.explanation: The Gα mutation in McCune-Albright syndrome occurs POSTZYGOTICALLY (somatically), producing a mosaic pattern of affected versus unaffected tissues — it is not a germline, heritable mutation.
items.reference: Genetic Syndromes Associated with Pituitary Tumors: "Hormonal hypersecretion results from constitutive cyclic AMP production caused by inactivation of the GTPase activity of Gα. The Gα mutations occur postzygotically, leading to a mosaic pattern of mutant expression."

items.statement: A serum prolactin level above 200 µg/L is almost always indicative of a prolactin-secreting pituitary adenoma.
items.explanation: Non-tumor causes of hyperprolactinemia (physiologic, drug-induced, hypothyroid, or stalk-compression) rarely exceed 100 µg/L. PRL levels >200 µg/L are almost invariably due to a prolactinoma.
items.reference: Hyperprolactinemia: "Hyperprolactinemia >200 µg/L almost invariably is indicative of a prolactin-secreting pituitary adenoma. Physiologic causes, hypothyroidism, and drug-induced hyperprolactinemia should be excluded before extensive evaluation."

items.statement: Cabergoline is generally better tolerated than bromocriptine but is not recommended when fertility is desired, due to its long half-life and high D2-receptor affinity.
items.explanation: Cabergoline's pharmacologic properties prolong drug exposure (including fetal exposure), and reproductive safety data are more limited than for bromocriptine, which is preferred when pregnancy is planned.
items.reference: Pregnancy: "Because cabergoline is long-acting with a high D2-receptor affinity, it is not recommended for use in women when fertility is desired."

items.statement: Large studies of more than 500 prolactinoma patients receiving standard cabergoline doses (up to 2 mg weekly) have shown a clearly increased incidence of cardiac valve regurgitation.
items.explanation: Valvular regurgitation risk is established at the much higher doses used for Parkinson's disease (≥3 mg/day). Large studies at standard prolactinoma doses (up to 2 mg/week) have shown NO increased incidence of valvulopathy, though a baseline echocardiogram is still considered prudent.
items.reference: Side Effects: "Studies analyzing >500 prolactinoma patients receiving recommended doses of cabergoline (up to 2 mg weekly) have shown no evidence for an increased incidence of valvular disorders."

items.statement: Acromegaly is associated with an increased risk of colon polyps and colorectal malignancy, warranting colonoscopic screening.
items.explanation: Colon polyps are diagnosed in up to one-third of acromegaly patients, with increased mortality from colonic malignancy — a clinically important reason to screen with colonoscopy.
items.reference: Acromegaly — Presentation and Diagnosis: "Acromegaly is associated with an increased risk of colon polyps and mortality from colonic malignancy; polyps are diagnosed in up to one-third of patients."

items.statement: Severe hypokalemic metabolic alkalosis (serum potassium <3.3 mmol/L) is more commonly seen in pituitary Cushing's disease than in ectopic ACTH-secreting tumors.
